timeline.event: Born on June 14 in Litchfield, Connecticut, USA.
Lived in Cincinnati, Ohio.
Wrote and published a children's geography book under her sister Catharine's name.
Married Calvin Stowe, a seminary professor.
Published the anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'.
Published the novel 'Dred'.
Published the novel 'The Minister's Wooing'.
Published the novel 'The Pearl of Orr's Island'.
Published 'Lady Byron Vindicated'.
Published 'Sam Lawson's Oldtown Fireside Stories'.
Published the novel 'Oldtown Folks'.
Died on July 1 at the age of 85 in Hartford.

keyAchievements.title: Writing 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'
keyAchievements.description: Published the anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin', which intensified the conflict of opinion between the North and South, ultimately leading to the Civil War.

keyAchievements.title: Writing a Children's Geography Book
keyAchievements.description: Wrote and published her first book, a children's geography book, in 1833 under her sister Catharine's name.

faq.question: Who was Harriet Beecher Stowe?
faq.answer: She was an American abolitionist and realist author, best known for her anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'.

faq.question: What was the impact of 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'?
faq.answer: The novel profoundly influenced the debate on slavery at the time and significantly widened the divide between the North and South, paving the way for the Civil War.

faq.question: Why did Harriet Beecher Stowe become interested in the abolitionist movement?
faq.answer: She witnessed the miserable living conditions of enslaved Black people during a trip to Kentucky, which fueled her passion for the abolitionist movement. Furthermore, the loss of her mother at a young age is said to have evoked a deep sense of empathy within her for enslaved boys and girls separated from their families.

timeline.event: Born on March 31 to a noble family of magistrates in La Haye en Touraine.
Entered the Jesuit-run Collège Royal Henry-Le-Grand at La Flèche and received education until 1614.
Obtained a Licence in law from the University of Poitiers.
Heard a difficult geometry problem from a Dutch university principal in Paris and solved it.
Traveled to the Netherlands and met Isaac Beeckman, and wrote his first short work, 'Compendium Musicæ'.
Enlisted in the army of Maximilian I of Bavaria, experienced the Battle of White Mountain, and had a dream in a barrack near Ulm that illuminated his life's path.
Discharged from the army and returned to France.
Began researching mathematics and natural sciences, especially optics, in Paris.
Wrote the unfinished treatise 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind'.
Wrote the fragment 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind', and moved to the Netherlands that winter seeking freedom for research and contemplation.
Wrote 'The World' (Le Monde), a book on natural science.
Published 'Discourse on the Method', anonymously appending three parts: Dioptrics, Meteorology, and Geometry.
Published 'Meditations on First Philosophy'.
Systematically organized his philosophy and published 'Principles of Philosophy' in Latin.

Letters sent to Elisabeth of the Palatinate were collected and published as his last book 'The Passions of the Soul', and he went to the Swedish royal palace at the invitation of Queen Christina of Sweden.
Caught pneumonia and passed away at the age of 54 in Stockholm on February 11.
His remains returned to Paris and were placed in the Abbey of Saint Genevieve-du-Mont.
The French government moved his remains to the Panthéon.
His remains were moved again and placed in the Abbey of Saint-Germain-des-Prés.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Foundation of Modern Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Analytic Geometry
keyAchievements.description: Devised the Cartesian coordinate system and founded analytic geometry, systematically integrating algebra and geometry.

keyAchievements.title: Established 'I think, therefore I am'
keyAchievements.description: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', he first established the fundamental principle of Enlightenment thought regarding the 'autonomous and rational subject' with 'I think, therefore I am'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Mind-Body Dualism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a dualism separating mind and matter, body and spirit, connecting the scientific view of nature with the metaphysics of the mind.

keyAchievements.title: Discovered the Law of Refraction of Light
keyAchievements.description: Through optical research, he independently proved the law of refraction of light, also known as 'Snell's Law'.

faq.question: Why is Descartes called the 'father of modern philosophy'?
faq.answer: He is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought, and he opened the path for modern mathematics by founding analytic geometry.

faq.question: What is Descartes' most famous proposition?
faq.answer: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', 'I think, therefore I am (Cogito, ergo sum)' is famous for first establishing the fundamental principle of the Enlightenment's 'autonomous and rational subject'.

faq.question: What were Descartes' religious beliefs?
faq.answer: He maintained his Roman Catholic faith throughout his life and claimed that one of the purposes of 'Meditations on First Philosophy' was to defend the Christian faith.

faq.question: What is Descartes' dualism?
faq.answer: He divided substance into two types: mental ('thinking thing') and material ('extended thing'), arguing that mind and body are two distinctly separate substances.

timeline.event: Born to a family of Portuguese-Jewish descent in Amsterdam, Netherlands.
When his father died, he took over the import goods shop run by his father at the age of 21.
Was excommunicated and cursed from the Jewish community on the pretext of criticizing Judaism and blaspheming God.
Wrote 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect' in Rijnsburg and published 'Principles of Cartesian Philosophy'.
Formed a relationship with Johan de Witt, a political leader of the Dutch Republic, in Voorburg.
Published 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' anonymously and settled in The Hague that year.
'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' was banned from publication by the Dutch government.
Began writing 'Tractatus Politicus'.
Passed away from tuberculosis at the age of 44.
Completed his lifelong work 'Ethics' but could not publish it during his lifetime; the first edition of 'Tractatus Politicus' was published.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote the 'Ethics'
keyAchievements.description: Partially accepting Descartes' foundationalism while adopting a philosophical stance against mind-body dualism in his masterpiece 'Ethics', which significantly influenced Western philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Unified Natural Substance and Moral Essence
keyAchievements.description: Revived the ideas of the ancient Stoic school in a modern sense, undertaking the philosophical task of unifying natural substance and moral essence.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of Conatus
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire or endeavor to preserve, develop, and perfect the self, and saw its full expression as happiness.

keyAchievements.title: Established Monistic Pantheism
keyAchievements.description: Viewed God and Nature as two names for the same substance, establishing a monistic pantheistic worldview with strong atheistic tendencies.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Modern Rationalist Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Recognized as one of the major rationalist philosophers of 17th-century Europe who laid the foundation for the 18th-century Enlightenment and modern biblical criticism.

faq.question: How was Spinoza treated in the Jewish community?
faq.answer: Although well-versed in Jewish culture, he was expelled from the Jewish community at age 23 due to his critical ideas, and all his works were placed on the Catholic Church's Index of Forbidden Books.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's main philosophical ideas?
faq.answer: He argued for monistic pantheism, viewing God and nature as two names for the same substance, and presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire to preserve, develop, and perfect the self.

faq.question: How did Spinoza make a living?
faq.answer: He barely made a living by grinding optical lenses and rejected any compensation or honors, including professorships, during his lifetime.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's representative works?
faq.answer: His lifelong masterpiece is 'Ethics, Demonstrated in Geometrical Order' ('Ethics'), and others include 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' and 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect'.

faq.question: Is the quote 'Even if the world were to end tomorrow, I would plant an apple tree today' by Spinoza?
faq.answer: These words were spoken by Martin Luther, and it is explicitly stated in the original text that it is unclear whether Spinoza actually said it.

timeline.event: Simone de Beauvoir is born in Paris, France.
Presents on Leibniz at the Sorbonne and passes the agrégation in philosophy in second place.
Publishes the novel She Came to Stay (L'Invitée).
Writes Pyrrhus and Cineas (Pyrrhus et Cinéas), an essay on existentialist ethics.
Publishes The Ethics of Ambiguity (Pour Une Morale de L'ambiguïté).
The Second Sex (Le Deuxième Sexe) is published in France.
Publishes the autobiography Memoirs of a Dutiful Daughter (Mémoires d'une jeune fille rangée).
Publishes the autobiography The Prime of Life (La Force de l'âge).
Publishes the autobiography Force of Circumstance (La Force des choses).
Writes the essay Old Age (La Vieillesse).
Signs the Manifesto of the 343 advocating for the legalization of abortion.
Publishes the autobiography All Said and Done (Tout compte fait).
Publishes When Things of the Spirit Come First (Quand prime le spirituel), containing stories of her experiences as a woman in her youth.
Writes Adieux: A Farewell to Sartre (La Cérémonie Des Adieux), covering Sartre's later years.
Dies of pneumonia and is buried in the Montparnasse Cemetery in Paris.

keyAchievements.title: Writer and Philosopher
keyAchievements.description: A prominent French writer and philosopher who wrote in various fields including novels, philosophical essays, biographies, and autobiographies.

keyAchievements.title: She Came to Stay
keyAchievements.description: A novelized chronicle of the complex relationship between herself, Sartre, Olga Kosakiewicz, and Wanda Kosakiewicz.

keyAchievements.title: The Mandarins
keyAchievements.description: A Goncourt Prize-winning novel set after World War II, depicting surrounding figures like Sartre and Nelson Algren.

keyAchievements.title: The Second Sex
keyAchievements.description: A monumental work containing an in-depth analysis of women's oppression, becoming a cornerstone of modern feminism.

keyAchievements.title: The Ethics of Ambiguity
keyAchievements.description: An accessible introduction to French existentialism, possessing a simplicity that contrasts with Sartre's more difficult works.

keyAchievements.title: Publication and Editing of Les Temps Modernes
keyAchievements.description: Co-founded the political magazine Les Temps Modernes with Sartre and Merleau-Ponty, and worked as an editor until shortly before her death.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Autobiographies
keyAchievements.description: Documented her life through four famous autobiographies, beginning with Memoirs of a Dutiful Daughter.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the French Women's Liberation Movement
keyAchievements.description: Actively participated in the French women's liberation movement in the 1970s and signed the Manifesto of the 343 advocating for the legalization of abortion.

faq.question: Who was Simone de Beauvoir?
faq.answer: She was a French writer and philosopher who wrote novels, as well as essays, biographies, and autobiographies on philosophy, politics, and social issues.

faq.question: What are her major works?
faq.answer: She wrote metaphysical novels such as She Came to Stay and The Mandarins, and in 1949 wrote The Second Sex, which became a cornerstone of modern feminism.

faq.question: What was Simone de Beauvoir's family background?
faq.answer: She was born to Georges de Beauvoir, a legal secretary, and Françoise Brasseur, and her younger sister Hélène de Beauvoir was a painter.

faq.question: What was her relationship with Jean-Paul Sartre?
faq.answer: She met Sartre at the University of Paris. In the 1929 philosophy agrégation, Sartre ranked first and she ranked second, and they maintained a complex lifelong relationship.

faq.question: What was the core argument in The Second Sex?
faq.answer: She argued that men had imposed a false aura of 'mystery' on women, turning them into the social 'Other', and adopting the maxim 'existence precedes essence', she posited that one is not born, but rather becomes, a woman.

faq.question: How did she participate in the women's liberation movement?
faq.answer: She was active in the French women's liberation movement in the 1970s and signed the Manifesto of the 343 in 1971, demanding the legalization of abortion, which was illegal in France at the time.

timeline.event: Galileo di Vincenzo Bonaiuti de' Galilei is born in Pisa, Tuscany, Italy.
Becomes a professor of mathematics at the University of Pisa.
Writes On Motion (De Motu).
Improves upon a 3x telescope invented in the Netherlands and demonstrates his first telescope to Venetian lawmakers.
Discovers the four largest moons of Jupiter using a telescope and publishes Sidereus Nuncius (Starry Messenger).
Accepts the invitation of Cosimo II, Grand Duke of Tuscany, moves to Florence, and continues his research.
Visits Rome and becomes a member of the Accademia dei Lincei.
Ordered by Cardinal Robert Bellarmine not to defend or teach Copernican astronomy.
Publishes The Assayer, attacking theories based on Aristotelian authority and advancing a mathematical system of scientific thought.
Publishes Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems, which leads to his trial by the Roman Inquisition.
Found guilty by the Inquisition, placed under house arrest, and all his works are placed on the Index of Forbidden Books.
Completes Discourses and Mathematical Demonstrations Relating to Two New Sciences, and becomes completely blind.
Galileo dies.
Pope John Paul II acknowledges that the Galileo trial was unjust and apologizes.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of the Telescope and Astronomical Observations
keyAchievements.description: Improved the telescope and discovered Jupiter's moons, lunar craters, and sunspots; his observation of the phases of Venus supported the Copernican heliocentric theory.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Laws of Motion
keyAchievements.description: Studied uniformly accelerated motion to establish kinematics, and established the concepts of velocity and acceleration, which laid the foundation for Newton's laws of motion.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Scientific Method
keyAchievements.description: Established universal mathematical laws and the quantitative analysis of empirical facts, leading to the beginning of physics in the modern sense.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Jupiter's Four Largest Moons
keyAchievements.description: Discovered Io, Europa, Callisto, and Ganymede, proving that not all celestial bodies revolve around the Earth, which contradicted the Aristotelian worldview.

faq.question: Did Galileo Galilei actually perform the drop experiment from the Leaning Tower of Pisa?
faq.answer: No, this experiment was a story invented by Viviani, Galileo's biographer and disciple, to elevate him; Galileo refuted Aristotle's theory through logical reasoning.

faq.question: Was Galileo a devout Catholic despite his conflicts with the Church?
faq.answer: Yes, despite his conflict with the Church, he was a devout Roman Catholic, to the point where he once hoped to become a Catholic priest.

faq.question: Is the anecdote true that Galileo muttered 'And yet it moves' after his Inquisition trial?
faq.answer: No, while this widely known anecdote illustrates his passion for seeking scientific truth, there is no reliable evidence that Galileo actually said it.

faq.question: What stance did the Vatican take on Galileo's trial concerning heliocentrism?
faq.answer: In 1992, Pope John Paul II acknowledged the injustice of the Galileo trial and issued an apology; earlier, in 1744, Galileo's book 'Dialogue' was removed from the Church's Index of Forbidden Books.

timeline.event: Born as the third son of Léopold Hugo in Besançon, eastern France.
Fascinated by reading and writing poetry at the Cordeliers boarding school in Paris, vowing to become a great French writer.
Published his first poetry collection, Odes et poésies diverses, and got married.
Published the novel Han d'Islande.
Awarded the Knight of the Legion of Honour by the French royal family in recognition of his merits as a writer.
Published the poetry collection Odes et Ballades and the novel Bug-Jargal.
Directly opposed classical literature and led romantic writers through the publication of the preface to his play Cromwell.
Published Les Orientales and Le Dernier jour d'un condamné.
The "Battle of Hernani" broke out between the romantic and classical schools around the premiere of the play Hernani.
Published the novel Notre-Dame de Paris, the poetry collection Les Feuilles d'automne, and the play Marion Delorme.
Published the play Le roi s'amuse.
Published the plays Lucrèce Borgia and Marie Tudor.
Published the poetry collection Les Chants du crépuscule.
Published the poetry collection Les Voix intérieures.
Published the verse play Ruy Blas.
Published the poetry collection Les Rayons et les Ombres.
Became a member of the Académie Française.
The play Les Burgraves was severely criticized, and his eldest daughter Léopoldine and her husband died in a boat capsizing accident.
Devoted himself to political activities, barely publishing any works.
Solidified his humanitarian tendencies, stimulated by surrounding circumstances including the February Revolution.
Rebelled against Napoleon III's coup d'état, was exiled abroad, and began about 19 years of exile.
Published the poetry collection Les Châtiments.
Published the poetry collection Les Contemplations.
Published the first series of the poetry collection La Légende des siècles.
Published the novel Les Misérables.
Published the novel Les Travailleurs de la mer.
Wrote Les Années funestes, depicting the reality of French workers.
Returned to Paris as a defender of republicanism with the fall of Napoleon III due to the Franco-Prussian War.
Published L'Année terrible.
Published the novel Quatrevingt-treize.
Published the second series of La Légende des siècles.
Wrote his will.
Published the third series of La Légende des siècles.
Died, and his remains were interred in the Panthéon in a state funeral.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Romantic Literature
keyAchievements.description: He presented the theory of romantic theater in the preface to his play Cromwell, won the "Battle of Hernani", and led romantic writers.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Masterpiece Novels
keyAchievements.description: He solidified his position as a novelist with Notre-Dame de Paris and wrote numerous masterpieces including Les Misérables during his exile.

keyAchievements.title: Social Criticism and Humanitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Through his works, he expressed love for poor workers, emphasized popular art, and solidified his humanitarian tendencies.

keyAchievements.title: Major Figure in the French Literary World
keyAchievements.description: He occupied an important position in the French literary world, receiving the Knight of the Legion of Honour in 1825 and becoming a member of the Académie Française in 1841.

faq.question: Who was Victor Hugo?
faq.answer: He was a French poet, novelist, and playwright.

faq.question: What impact did Victor Hugo have on romanticism?
faq.answer: He presented the theory of romantic theater through the preface to his play Cromwell, won the "Battle of Hernani", and led romantic writers.

faq.question: What are Victor Hugo's representative works?
faq.answer: They include the novels Notre-Dame de Paris and Les Misérables.

faq.question: Why was Victor Hugo in exile?
faq.answer: He rebelled against Napoleon III's coup d'état in 1851, was exiled abroad, and began about 19 years of exile.

faq.question: What was Victor Hugo's relationship with Catholicism?
faq.answer: His works were banned by the Catholic Church, and he could not reconcile with Catholicism, even refusing the church's prayers.

faq.question: When did Victor Hugo die, and where is he buried?
faq.answer: He died on May 22, 1885, and his remains were interred in the Panthéon.

timeline.event: Frédéric François Chopin was born.
Chopin's family moved to Warsaw.
Warsaw became part of the constitutional Kingdom of Poland.
Received his first professional music education from Wojciech Żywny.
Began holding public concerts at age 7 and composed two polonaises.
Composed the Polonaise in A-flat major and dedicated it to Żywny.
Performed his own improvisations and part of Moscheles's concerto on the aeolomelodicon.
Performed his Rondo Op. 1 (his first commercially published work) at an aeolopantaleon concert.
Attended the Warsaw Lyceum and studied music theory, figured bass, and composition at the Warsaw Conservatory for 3 years.
Shortly after his youngest sister Emilia died, the family moved to Krakowskie Przedmieście.
Visited Berlin with a family friend.
Composed Souvenir de Paganini after hearing Niccolò Paganini play the violin.
Debuted in Vienna, holding two piano concerts and receiving favorable reviews.
Premiered Piano Concerto No. 2 in F minor, Op. 21.
Left Warsaw.
The November Uprising broke out in Warsaw.
Expressed anguish in his diary upon hearing news of the uprising's suppression while traveling from Vienna to Paris.
Arrived in Paris.
Robert Schumann declared 'Hats off, gentlemen, a genius' in a review of the Op. 2 Variations.
Held his Paris debut concert to universal acclaim.
Published the Études Op. 10.
Attended the Lower Rhenish Music Festival with Hiller and met Felix Mendelssohn.
Went to Karlovy Vary to spend his last time with his parents.
Obtained French citizenship.
Met George Sand at a party hosted by Marie d'Agoult.
Stayed with the Wodziński family in Marienbad and Dresden, and proposed to Maria.
Visited London secretly with piano manufacturer Camille Pleyel.
Published the Études Op. 25.
Became lovers with George Sand.
Spent the winter with Sand in Majorca.
Published the Preludes Op. 28.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 2, Op. 35.
Headed to Sand's estate in Nohant to spend the summer.
Last public joint appearance with Liszt.
Wrote a letter to Grzymała about his deteriorating health after a Paris recital.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 3, Op. 58.
The relationship between Sand and Chopin deteriorated.
His relationship with Sand ended after a heated exchange of letters.
Held his last Paris concert with cellist Auguste Franchomme.
Left for London during the French Revolution of 1848.
Queen Victoria and Prince Albert attended his first London performance at Stafford House.
Wrote his last will in Edinburgh.
Held his last public concert at the Guildhall in London.
His older sister Ludwika came to Paris with her husband and daughter.
Died in Paris at the age of 39.
Funeral held at the Madeleine Church in Paris.
Chopin's tombstone was installed.
His older sister Ludwika took Chopin's heart back to Poland.
Julian Fontana compiled and published 23 unpublished piano pieces with 8 additional opus numbers (Op. 66–73).
17 Polish songs were published as Op. 74.
Chopin's music was used in the ballet Les Sylphides (Chopiniana).
Claude Debussy dedicated his piano études to the memory of Chopin.
Results of a visual examination of Chopin's preserved heart were published in the American Journal of Medicine.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Composer
keyAchievements.description: Maintained worldwide fame with his poetic genius based on a professional technique unmatched by anyone of his time.

keyAchievements.title: Specializing in Solo Piano Music
keyAchievements.description: Composed primarily solo piano music, and all his known works feature the piano.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Instrumental Ballade
keyAchievements.description: Created the instrumental ballade as a new musical genre.

keyAchievements.title: Making the Scherzo a Concert Piece
keyAchievements.description: Was the first to compose scherzos as individual concert pieces.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Prelude Genre
keyAchievements.description: Established essentially a new genre with his free cycle of preludes (Op. 28).

keyAchievements.title: Utilizing the Poetic Potential of Études
keyAchievements.description: Utilized the poetic potential of the concept of the concert étude in his two sets of études (Op. 10, Op. 25).

keyAchievements.title: Elevating the Status of the Mazurka
keyAchievements.description: Put the mazurka on the European musical map.

keyAchievements.title: New Standards for the Polonaise Form
keyAchievements.description: Set new standards for music in the polonaise form.

keyAchievements.title: Deepening the Nocturne Genre
keyAchievements.description: Elevated the nocturne to a deeper level of sophistication.

keyAchievements.title: Introducing Nationalist Consciousness into Music
keyAchievements.description: Is evaluated as having introduced a new nationalist consciousness into music.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Piano and Composition Schools
keyAchievements.description: Was called the founder of piano and composition schools.

faq.question: When was Chopin born?
faq.answer: Frédéric François Chopin was born on March 1, 1810.

faq.question: What was Chopin's real name?
faq.answer: His real name was Fryderyk Franciszek Chopin.

faq.question: What instrument did Chopin primarily compose for?
faq.answer: Chopin composed primarily solo piano music, and the piano appears in all his works.

faq.question: How many public performances did Chopin give in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He gave a minimal number of public performances, slightly over 30, preferring the more intimate atmosphere of salons.

faq.question: How did Chopin make a living in Paris?
faq.answer: He made a living by selling his compositions and giving piano lessons, which were highly popular at the time.

faq.question: What was George Sand's relationship to Chopin?
faq.answer: George Sand was a French author and Chopin's lover; they became lovers in July 1838.

faq.question: What was the relationship between Chopin and Franz Liszt?
faq.answer: The two were friends and respected each other, but their friendship was unstable and had the characteristics of a love-hate relationship.

faq.question: What was the cause of Chopin's death?
faq.answer: His death certificate listed the cause of death as tuberculosis, while recent research suggests it was highly likely a rare pericarditis caused by chronic tuberculosis complications.

faq.question: Where is Chopin's heart preserved?
faq.answer: His heart was removed by a doctor, preserved in alcohol in an urn, and taken back to Poland by his older sister Ludwika in 1850.

faq.question: Did Chopin give descriptive titles to his works?
faq.answer: With the exception of his Funeral March, the composer never named his instrumental pieces beyond genre and number.

timeline.event: Born into an artisan family in the Katsushika district of Edo (Childhood name: Tokitaro).
Began drawing around age 6.
Worked at a bookstore and lending library at age 12.
Worked as an apprentice woodcarver at age 14.
Entered the studio of Katsukawa Shunshō at age 18.
Received the name Shunrō from his master and published his first series of prints of Kabuki actors.
Produced Fireworks at Ryōgoku Bridge in Edo.
Death of his master Katsukawa Shunshō.
Second marriage.
Passed the name Tawaraya Sōri to his pupil, began working as an independent artist, and used the name "Hokusai Tomisa".
Began using the name "Katsushika Hokusai", published the landscape print series Famous Views of the Eastern Capital and Eight Views of Edo, and started taking on pupils.
Painted a massive portrait of the Buddhist monk Daruma, about 200 square meters in size, during an Edo festival.
Collaborated with popular novelist Kyokutei Bakin to produce a series of illustrated novels.
Produced illustrations for the fantasy novel Strange Tales of the Crescent Moon.
Changed his name to Taito at age 51 and began producing Hokusai Manga and various etehon (art manuals).
Produced Quick Lessons in Simplified Drawing.
The first volume of Hokusai Manga was published and became a huge success.
Painted a giant portrait of Daruma outside the Hongan-ji Nagoya Betsuin in Nagoya.
Changed his name to Iitsu and began gaining a solid reputation as an artist throughout Japan.
Produced his masterpiece, Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced Oceans of Wisdom (Shika Shashin-kyō).
Began working under the name "Gakyō Rōjin Manji" (The Old Man Mad About Art) and produced One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced his last print series, One Hundred Poems Explained by the Nurse.
A fire destroyed his studio and much of his work.
Visited Obuse in Shinano Province at age 83 and stayed there for several years.
Painted Chinese lions (shishi) every morning as a talisman to ward off bad luck.
Painted Dragon Flying in Clouds above Mount Fuji and Tiger in Snow.
Died and was buried at Seikyō-ji Temple in Taito Ward, Tokyo.

keyAchievements.title: Production of Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced this ukiyo-e series, which includes the iconic The Great Wave off Kanagawa, earning immense fame both domestically and internationally.

keyAchievements.title: Expanding Ukiyo-e Subjects
keyAchievements.description: He played a crucial role in broadening the subjects of ukiyo-e, which had primarily focused on courtesans and actors, to include landscapes, plants, and animals.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Output
keyAchievements.description: Working tirelessly from childhood until his death, he produced over 30,000 paintings, sketches, woodblock prints, and images for picture books.

keyAchievements.title: Innovative Composition and Depiction Skills
keyAchievements.description: He is considered one of the greatest masters in art history due to his innovative compositions and outstanding depictive skills.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Hokusai Manga
keyAchievements.description: He produced Hokusai Manga, containing thousands of drawings, which immediately became a huge success and was also used as a drawing manual.

keyAchievements.title: Experimentation with Western Perspective
keyAchievements.description: Among Japanese artists, he early on experimented with Western perspective, applying techniques to express depth and volume in his works.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European Art
keyAchievements.description: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

keyAchievements.title: Production of One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced another important series, One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji, evaluated as "the greatest masterpiece among his landscape collections."

faq.question: Who was Katsushika Hokusai?
faq.answer: He was a Japanese ukiyo-e painter and printmaker of the Edo period, who played a crucial role in expanding the subjects of ukiyo-e to landscapes, plants, and animals.

faq.question: What is Hokusai's most famous work?
faq.answer: The Great Wave off Kanagawa, included in his ukiyo-e series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, is his most iconic work.

faq.question: What influence did Hokusai have on Western art?
faq.answer: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

faq.question: How many names did Hokusai use in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He is known to have used at least 30 names during his lifetime, closely linked to changes in his artwork and style.

faq.question: When did Hokusai die?
faq.answer: He died on May 10, 1849, showing a passion for creating better works even on his deathbed.

timeline.event: Died

keyAchievements.title: Succession and Development of Daoist Thought
keyAchievements.description: Succeeded and developed Laozi's thought, becoming a representative figure of Daoism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of Myriad Things Monism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the monism of all things.

keyAchievements.title: Argument for Return to Nature and Nothingness
keyAchievements.description: Inheriting Laozi's thoughts, he argued for a return to nature and a return to nothingness.

keyAchievements.title: Pursuit of Individual Peace of Mind
keyAchievements.description: Unlike Laozi, he devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of a Philosophy for an Ideal Life
keyAchievements.description: He viewed the ideal life as one that discards the physical and mental roots of anxiety, reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility' to achieve freedom and independence.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Epistemology
keyAchievements.description: Established an epistemology that conceptual recognition and value judgments are meaningless, and that only thorough ignorance is correct.

faq.question: What was Zhuangzi's real name?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi's real name was Zhou (周).

faq.question: Whose thoughts did Zhuangzi succeed and develop?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi succeeded and developed the thoughts of Laozi.

faq.question: What government position did Zhuangzi hold?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi became an official of the lacquer tree garden in Meng.

faq.question: Did Zhuangzi accept the offer of King Wei of Chu?
faq.answer: King Wei of Chu requested him to assist in politics, but Zhuangzi refused.

faq.question: What is Zhuangzi's ideal life?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi viewed the ideal life as discarding the physical and mental roots of anxiety and reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility'. Through this, he argued, one could follow the laws of nature and attain freedom and independence without being infringed upon by anything.

faq.question: How do Zhuangzi's thoughts differ from Laozi's?
faq.answer: While Laozi had some interest in the reality of politics and society, Zhuangzi devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

timeline.event: Born near present-day Melmoth, KwaZulu-Natal, as the illegitimate son of Zulu Chief Senzangakhona.
Took the Zulu throne following the death of his father, with the backing of the Mthethwa paramount chief Dingiswayo.
After Dingiswayo's death, Shaka defeated the rival Ndwandwe clan at the Battle of Gqokli Hill, establishing Zulu dominance.
His mother, Nandi, died, triggering a period of extreme and brutal mourning enforced by Shaka.
Assassinated by his half-brothers Dingane and Mhlangana at his capital, kwaDukuza.
Assassinated by his half-brothers

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Zulu Empire
keyAchievements.description: Transformed the small Zulu clan into a massive, militaristic empire that dominated southern Africa.

keyAchievements.title: Military Innovations
keyAchievements.description: Revolutionized regional warfare by introducing the iklwa (a short stabbing spear), the large cowhide shield, and the highly effective 'buffalo horns' formation.

keyAchievements.title: Social Integration
keyAchievements.description: Integrated conquered tribes into the Zulu nation, replacing traditional tribal loyalties with loyalty to the king and the age-grade military regiments (amabutho).

keyAchievements.title: Development of the 'bull horn' tactic
keyAchievements.description: Developed the 'bull horn' formation tactic that annihilated Zwide's army at the Battle of Gqokli Hill.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of hegemony based on military power
keyAchievements.description: Expanded his hegemony by defeating rivals and incorporating their scattered remnants into his own army based on military power.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of the Nguni shield
keyAchievements.description: It is believed that he introduced a larger and heavier version of the Nguni shield.

keyAchievements.title: Training in shield tactics
keyAchievements.description: Believed to have taught warriors the tactic of using the left side of their shield to hook the enemy's shield to the right, exposing their ribs.

keyAchievements.title: Forced march training
keyAchievements.description: Frequently subjected his troops to forced marches across hot, rocky terrain at a rapid pace.

keyAchievements.title: Youth logistical support system
keyAchievements.description: Recruited boys over the age of six as apprentice warriors (udibi) to handle logistical support such as carrying food, pots, mats, and spare weapons.

keyAchievements.title: Organization of age-grade regiments
keyAchievements.description: Organized different age groups into regiments, stationed them in special military kraals, and gave them unique names and insignia.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of military discipline
keyAchievements.description: Instilled ruthless determination in his army by drilling into them that a cruel fate awaited those who lost courage or were defeated in battle.

keyAchievements.title: Incorporation of conquered tribes
keyAchievements.description: Incorporated the remnants of conquered tribes into his army and used them to conquer other tribes.

faq.question: What is the 'Mfecane'?
faq.answer: The Mfecane (The Crushing) refers to a period of widespread chaos and warfare among indigenous ethnic communities in southern Africa in the early 19th century. While Shaka's expansions contributed to this, modern historians debate the extent to which he was the primary cause versus other factors like drought and colonial pressures.

faq.question: Was Shaka as brutal as he is often portrayed?
faq.answer: He was a brilliant but ruthless leader. Much of his 'bloodthirsty' reputation was exaggerated by early European writers to justify colonialism. However, his harsh discipline and the mass executions following his mother's death demonstrate he could be extremely cruel.

faq.question: What was the 'buffalo horns' formation?
faq.answer: It was a military tactic Shaka perfected. It consisted of a strong center (the 'chest') to pin the enemy, while two flanking wings (the 'horns') encircled them, and a reserve force (the 'loins') waited behind to pursue fleeing enemies or reinforce weak spots.

faq.question: How did Shaka Zulu die?
faq.answer: He was assassinated in September 1828 by his half-brothers Dingane kaSenzangakhona and Mhlangana, and Mbopa kaSithayi.

faq.question: What was Shaka Zulu's role in the Mfecane?
faq.answer: Shaka's reign coincided with the Mfecane period, and although his military expansion caused massive migrations and turmoil, his exact role remains controversial.
